explicit_text: I was born in Chicago in 1916, a city where many Jewish immigrants from Lithuania had settled. My grandparents, Solomon and Zlata Goodman, made their home there after arriving in 1914, just before World War I began. Chicago was the place where my family started their new life in America, and it shaped much of our early experiences as immigrants.
implicit_text: I was born in a major Midwestern city in 1916, a place where many Jewish immigrants from Lithuania had settled. My grandparents, Solomon and Zlata Goodman, made their home there after arriving in 1914, just before World War I began. This city was where my family started their new life in America, and it shaped much of our early experiences as immigrants.

explicit_text: My family originally came from Lithuania, fleeing oppression and fear as Jewish people in Eastern Europe. My great-grandfather Solomon Goodman left Lithuania in the early 1900s seeking a better life in the 'new world.' Both my grandparents and my husband Haskell Friedman's family were Lithuanian Jewish immigrants who found refuge and opportunity in the United States.
implicit_text: My family originally came from a Baltic country, fleeing oppression and fear as Jewish people in Eastern Europe. My great-grandfather Solomon Goodman left that region in the early 1900s seeking a better life in the 'new world.' Both my grandparents and my husband Haskell Friedman's family were Jewish immigrants from that area who found refuge and opportunity in the United States.

explicit_text: After marrying Haskell Friedman in 1940, we moved from Chicago to Janesville, Wisconsin, a town mostly populated by Norwegian immigrants. There, we ran the general store for 35 years and became part of the community, even adopting some Norwegian customs like clothing and baking. Janesville was where we raised our family and balanced our Jewish traditions with the local culture.
implicit_text: After marrying Haskell Friedman in 1940, we moved from a large Midwestern city to a smaller town in Wisconsin, mostly populated by Norwegian immigrants. There, we ran the general store for 35 years and became part of the community, even adopting some Norwegian customs like clothing and baking. This Wisconsin town was where we raised our family and balanced our Jewish traditions with the local culture.

explicit_text: Solomon Goodman was my great-grandfather who left Lithuania in the early 1900s to escape persecution and seek a better life in America. He worked hard to establish himself and sent for his family, who arrived in 1914 just before World War I. Solomon settled in Chicago, where he raised his family, including my grandmother Bella, helping to lay the foundation for our family's American story.
implicit_text: My great-grandfather was a man who left the Baltic region in the early 1900s to escape persecution and seek a better life in America. He worked hard to establish himself and sent for his family, who arrived in 1914 just before World War I. He settled in a major Midwestern city, where he raised his family, including my grandmother Bella, helping to lay the foundation for our family's American story.

explicit_text: My great-grandparents left Lithuania in July 1914 on one of the last ships before World War I began, escaping the turmoil in Europe. The timing was critical because the war made it much harder for Jewish families like mine to find safety. Their arrival in the United States just before the war allowed our family to survive and build a new life away from the conflict.
implicit_text: My great-grandparents left the Baltic region in July 1914 on one of the last ships before the global conflict began, escaping the turmoil in Europe. The timing was critical because the war made it much harder for Jewish families like mine to find safety. Their arrival in the United States just before the war allowed our family to survive and build a new life away from the conflict.

explicit_text: Growing up, my grandmother Bella spoke Yiddish fluently alongside English, which helped her translate life in America for her family. Yiddish was an important part of our cultural heritage, connecting us to our Lithuanian Jewish roots. Bella loved singing in Yiddish and it was a key part of her identity as she navigated life between two worlds.
implicit_text: Growing up, my grandmother Bella spoke a Jewish language fluently alongside English, which helped her translate life in America for her family. This language was an important part of our cultural heritage, connecting us to our Baltic Jewish roots. Bella loved singing in this language and it was a key part of her identity as she navigated life between two worlds.

explicit_text: Like many families, my grandmother Bella and her family struggled through the Great Depression, which made life very difficult in the 1930s. Despite these hardships, they persevered and continued to work hard to support each other. The Depression was a defining period that tested their resilience as immigrants in America.
implicit_text: Like many families, my grandmother Bella and her family struggled through the severe economic downturn of the 1930s, which made life very difficult. Despite these hardships, they persevered and continued to work hard to support each other. This period was a defining time that tested their resilience as immigrants in America.

explicit_text: I married Haskell Friedman in 1940; he came from a family of Jewish immigrants from Lithuania, just like my own. Together, we moved to Janesville, Wisconsin, where we raised our family and ran the town's general store for 35 years. Haskell and I worked hard to blend our Jewish traditions with the local Norwegian community, creating a unique life for our children.
implicit_text: I married a man in 1940 who came from a family of Jewish immigrants from the Baltic region, just like my own. Together, we moved to a Wisconsin town where we raised our family and ran the town's general store for 35 years. We worked hard to blend our Jewish traditions with the local Norwegian community, creating a unique life for our children.

explicit_text: In Janesville, Wisconsin, we lived among mostly Norwegian immigrants and embraced many of their customs, like wearing Norwegian clothing for festivals and baking their traditional cookies. Though we maintained our Jewish faith, we became part of the Norwegian community through our store and social life. This blending of cultures enriched our family's experience in America.
implicit_text: In the Wisconsin town where we lived, the majority of residents were immigrants from Scandinavia, and we embraced many of their customs, like wearing their traditional clothing for festivals and baking their cookies. Though we maintained our Jewish faith, we became part of this Scandinavian community through our store and social life. This blending of cultures enriched our family's experience in America.

explicit_text: Judaism was central to our family's identity; even while living in a predominantly Norwegian town, we made sure our children received a Jewish education. We regularly drove to the nearest synagogue to keep our religious traditions alive and stayed connected with Jewish friends and relatives in Wisconsin and Illinois. Maintaining our faith helped us preserve our heritage and community bonds.
implicit_text: Our religion was central to our family's identity; even while living in a predominantly Scandinavian town, we made sure our children received a religious education. We regularly drove to the nearest place of worship to keep our traditions alive and stayed connected with friends and relatives who shared our faith in Wisconsin and Illinois. Maintaining our beliefs helped us preserve our heritage and community bonds.

explicit_text: The Holocaust was a tragic event that deeply affected my family’s history, as many Jewish relatives in Europe were lost. My great-grandparents' immigration to the U.S. before World War I likely saved their lives, while others who were turned away from America in 1939 faced deadly consequences. Remembering the Holocaust reminds me why welcoming immigrants is so important today.

explicit_text: The United States has been the place where my family found safety, opportunity, and the freedom to practice our religion without fear. From my great-grandfather Solomon Goodman arriving in the early 1900s, to my grandparents settling in Chicago and later Janesville, the U.S. gave us a chance to build a new life. Our story reflects the immigrant experience of hard work, cultural blending, and hope for future generations.
